section.title: To Us a Child Is Born
section.sectionRationale: This short, luminous oracle is the high point of the Immanuel section: the darkness and gloom of 8:22 is suddenly answered by a great light dawning on Galilee of the nations, and the child of Immanuel now receives a fourfold throne name, Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace, whose government of justice and righteousness will never end.
section.thesis: Where the previous chapter ended in darkness, God answers with the promise of a child, born for us, given to us, whose divine titles and everlasting, just reign will finally undo every yoke of oppression, guaranteed by the zeal of the LORD of Hosts Himself.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the great light dawning on a people who walked in darkness, and the child given whose fourfold name and everlasting government secure that light forever.

verses.text: Nevertheless, there will be no more gloom for those in distress. In the past He humbled the land of Zebulun and the land of Naphtali, but in the future He will honor the Way of the Sea, beyond the Jordan, Galilee of the nations:
The people walking in darkness have seen a great {{or|light}}; on those living in the land of the shadow of death, a light has dawned.

You have enlarged the nation and increased its joy. The people rejoice before You as they rejoice at harvest time, 
as men rejoice in dividing the plunder.
For as in the day of Midian You have shattered the yoke of their burden, the bar across their shoulders, and the rod of their oppressor.

For every trampling boot of battle and every garment rolled in blood 
will be burned as fuel for the fire.

units.label: The child given
units.note: Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace

verses.text:
For unto us a child is born, unto us a son is given, 
and the government will be upon His shoulders. And He will be called {{peleyoetz|Wonderful Counselor}}, {{elgibbor|Mighty God}}, 
{{aviad|Everlasting Father}}, {{sarshalom|Prince of Peace}}.

or.gloss: Light, the very first thing God speaks into being in Genesis 1:3. Here light dawns on a people in deepest darkness and the shadow of death, an image Matthew 4:15-16 quotes directly as fulfilled when Jesus began His ministry in Galilee.

peleyoetz.gloss: Wonderful Counselor, combining pele, a wonder or extraordinary marvel usually reserved for God's own works, with yo'etz, one who counsels or plans. This child's wisdom and plans are not merely wise but divinely wondrous.

elgibbor.gloss: Mighty God, using El, the ordinary Hebrew word for God, paired with gibbor, mighty or warrior-strong, the very same phrase Isaiah later uses for the LORD Himself in 10:21. This is a direct, explicit claim to the child's deity, not a merely honorific title.

aviad.gloss: Everlasting Father, literally Father of eternity, describing not a confusion of persons within the Trinity but the child's eternal, fatherly care and unending relationship to His people, a King who rules with the enduring, provident love of a father forever.

sarshalom.gloss: Prince of Peace, sar meaning prince or ruler and shalom meaning peace in its fullest sense, wholeness, wellbeing, and right relationship, not merely the absence of conflict. His government secures shalom that never diminishes.

mishpat3.gloss: Justice, the right ordering of community and right judgment. The child's everlasting throne is established and sustained by this quality, the very justice Israel's own leaders had so badly failed to provide in chapters 1 and 3.

tzedaqah3.gloss: Righteousness, right conduct flowing from right relationship with God. Paired here with mishpat, justice, as the twin foundation of the coming King's everlasting reign on David's throne.

qinah.gloss: Zeal, jealous, passionate commitment. The LORD's own qinah, not merely human effort or political circumstance, guarantees that this promise of an everlasting, just, and peaceful kingdom will actually be accomplished.

basic.context: After the darkness and gloom that closed the previous chapter, Isaiah suddenly announces good news: a great light has dawned on the very region, Galilee, that had suffered most under foreign oppression. This light is a child, born and given to His people, who will bear the government on His shoulders and carry a fourfold name describing wisdom, deity, eternal fatherly care, and lasting peace, ruling David's throne forever with justice and righteousness.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a genuine messianic prophecy whose titles go well beyond what any merely human king could bear, especially Mighty God and Everlasting Father, language the Old Testament reserves for deity itself. Matthew 4:12-16 explicitly identifies the fulfillment of the Galilee light with the start of Jesus's ministry, and the everlasting Davidic throne described here awaited a King who, unlike every prior son of David, would actually reign forever, exactly what the New Testament proclaims of the risen Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: A great or, light, dawns on people walking in darkness, an image Matthew quotes directly of Jesus's Galilean ministry. The child's fourfold name stacks up staggering claims: pele yo'etz, Wonderful Counselor, whose plans are divinely wondrous; el gibbor, Mighty God, using the plain Hebrew word for God Himself; avi ad, Everlasting Father, describing eternal, fatherly care; and sar shalom, Prince of Peace, whose reign secures shalom, complete wellbeing, that never ends, established through mishpat and tzedaqah, justice and righteousness, and guaranteed by the LORD's own qinah, zeal.
basic.typology: The whole passage is typological in its trajectory: an immediate hope for relief from Assyrian oppression in Isaiah's day opens onto an everlasting kingdom no merely human king ever achieved, a pattern of near and far fulfillment that finds its full resolution only in Christ, the true and final Son of David whose kingdom, unlike Solomon's or Hezekiah's, truly has no end.
basic.christ: Matthew 4:14-16 quotes Isaiah 9:1-2 directly, declaring it fulfilled when Jesus began His public ministry in Galilee, the light dawning exactly where Isaiah said it would. The fourfold name, especially Mighty God and Everlasting Father, is an explicit Old Testament claim to the coming King's full deity, centuries before the incarnation, and Luke 1:32-33 echoes verse 7's promise of an endless Davidic reign directly in the angel's announcement to Mary about Jesus.
basic.god: God Himself guarantees this promise not through human effort but through His own zeal, His passionate, jealous commitment to accomplish what He has spoken. The titles given to this child claim nothing less than that God's own government, wisdom, might, fatherly care, and peace, would come to dwell among His people in a single person, on David's own throne.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 4:14-16 explicitly quotes this passage as fulfilled in Jesus's Galilean ministry. Luke 1:32-33 echoes the promise of an endless reign on David's throne in Gabriel's announcement to Mary. 2 Samuel 7:12-16 provides the original Davidic covenant promise of an everlasting throne that this oracle develops further. John 8:12 has Jesus explicitly claim to be the light of the world, drawing on exactly this kind of light-in-darkness imagery.
basic.oneStory: Isaiah 9:1-7 is one of the clearest bridges in the Old Testament between Israel's immediate hopes and the whole Bible's ultimate hope: a child born who is somehow also Mighty God, whose government of justice, righteousness, and peace will never end. That child is Jesus, in whom the light promised to a darkened Galilee, and the everlasting throne promised to David, are both finally and fully realized.

context.literary: The chapter break between 8 and 9 is unfortunate, since 9:1 begins with 'nevertheless,' directly answering the utter darkness that closed chapter 8; the light-in-darkness reversal is the hinge of the whole oracle. The passage builds through images of harvest joy and shattered yokes before climaxing in the fourfold throne name and the guarantee of the LORD's own zeal, forming one of the most compact and theologically dense birth announcements in all of Scripture.
context.historical: Zebulun and Naphtali, the regions named, were the northern tribal territories first and hardest hit by Assyrian incursions, later becoming the Assyrian province of Galilee, exactly the region where, centuries later, Jesus of Nazareth would grow up and begin His public ministry, a detail Matthew highlights as directly significant. The oracle likely responds to the same historical distress addressed throughout chapters 7-8, offering hope beyond the immediate Assyrian crisis.
context.canonical: This passage stands alongside Isaiah 7:14 and Isaiah 11:1-5 as one of the three great Immanuel-block messianic texts, each developing the coming child's identity further: the sign of His presence (7:14), the fullness of His divine and royal titles (9:6-7), and the character of His righteous, Spirit-empowered reign (11:1-5). Together they form the backbone of Isaiah's messianic hope.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To answer the darkness and gloom of the preceding oracle with the promise of a light-bringing, divine child whose everlasting, just, and peaceful reign on David's throne would finally and fully resolve the oppression Isaiah's audience knew firsthand.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage is prophetic promise, not prescription; it does not instruct believers in a program of action but announces what God Himself will accomplish through this coming King. The enduring truth is that God's justice, righteousness, and peace are ultimately secured not by human political achievement but by the reign of the divine Son He would send.

debates.question: Does 'Mighty God' in verse 6 make an explicit, direct claim to the child's full deity, or does it describe an unusually exalted human king using hyperbolic royal language common in the ancient Near East?

views.label: A direct, explicit claim to deity
views.case: El gibbor, Mighty God, is the exact same phrase Isaiah uses of the LORD Himself in 10:21, and paired with 'Everlasting Father,' the titles go well beyond typical ancient royal hyperbole into unambiguous divine language that the New Testament sees fulfilled precisely in the incarnate Christ.

views.label: Elevated royal language within its ancient Near Eastern context
views.case: Ancient Near Eastern royal ideology sometimes used exalted, even divine-sounding titles for human kings without claiming literal deity, so some read these titles as extraordinary praise for an ideal Davidic king rather than a strict ontological claim.

debates.bearing: Even interpreters cautious about reading full Trinitarian theology directly into an eighth century BC text agree the titles are extraordinary beyond any merely human ruler, and the New Testament's application of this passage's trajectory to Jesus, true God and true man, resolves the tension the Old Testament text itself leaves remarkably open.

study.originalLanguages: A great or, light, breaks upon a people walking in darkness, language Matthew 4:14-16 quotes directly of Jesus's Galilean ministry. The child's name stacks divine and royal claims: pele yo'etz, Wonderful Counselor, whose plans are wondrous beyond ordinary human wisdom; el gibbor, Mighty God, the very phrase Isaiah later applies to the LORD Himself in 10:21; avi ad, Everlasting Father, eternal fatherly care rather than a confusion of Trinitarian persons; and sar shalom, Prince of Peace, whose reign secures shalom, full wellbeing, forever, established through mishpat and tzedaqah, justice and righteousness, and guaranteed absolutely by the LORD's own qinah, zeal.

typology.type: The light dawning on Galilee of the nations, Isaiah 9:1-2
typology.fulfillment: The start of Jesus's public ministry in Galilee, quoted directly in Matthew 4:14-16.

typology.type: The child given an everlasting throne of David, Isaiah 9:6-7
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, whose kingdom, unlike every prior Davidic king, truly has no end, Luke 1:32-33.

study.christ: Matthew 4:14-16 explicitly quotes Isaiah 9:1-2 as fulfilled the moment Jesus began His public ministry in Galilee, the very region this oracle names. The fourfold title, especially Mighty God and Everlasting Father, is a direct Old Testament claim to the coming King's full deity centuries before the incarnation, and Luke 1:32-33 echoes this passage's promise of a never-ending Davidic reign directly in the angel Gabriel's announcement to Mary that her son, Jesus, would reign on David's throne forever.
study.god: God's own zeal, not mere human effort or favorable political circumstance, guarantees the fulfillment of this astonishing promise. In naming the coming child with titles claiming wisdom, deity, eternal fatherhood, and peace, God announces that His own character and government would come to dwell among His people embodied in a single person on David's throne.

crossReferences.note: Explicitly quotes this passage as fulfilled when Jesus began His ministry in Galilee, the region of darkness now seeing light.
Gabriel's announcement to Mary echoes this passage's promise of an endless reign on David's throne, fulfilled in Jesus.
The original Davidic covenant promising an everlasting throne, which this oracle develops and personalizes in a single coming child.
Jesus explicitly claims to be the light of the world, drawing on the same light-in-darkness imagery this passage announces.

study.oneStory: Isaiah 9:1-7 bridges Israel's immediate hopes for relief from oppression and the whole Bible's ultimate hope for a divine, everlasting King. That child, given titles no mere human king could bear, is Jesus, in whom the light promised to darkened Galilee and the everlasting throne promised to David are both fully and finally realized.

quiz.q: What does the 'great light' of verses 1-2 specifically fulfill, according to the New Testament?

options.text: The beginning of Jesus's public ministry in Galilee, quoted directly in Matthew 4:14-16.
options.note: Matthew explicitly cites Isaiah 9:1-2 as fulfilled when Jesus began teaching in Galilee.

options.text: The birth of John the Baptist.
options.note: Matthew ties this specific prophecy to Jesus's Galilean ministry, not John's birth.

options.text: The rebuilding of the Jerusalem temple after the exile.
options.note: The New Testament applies this text specifically to Jesus's ministry, not the temple's reconstruction.

options.text: The conversion of the Assyrian empire.
options.note: The light dawns specifically on Galilee's own people, not on Assyria as a nation.

quiz.q: Why is the title 'Mighty God' in verse 6 theologically significant?

options.text: El gibbor is the same phrase Isaiah uses of the LORD Himself elsewhere, an explicit claim to the child's full deity.
options.note: Isaiah 10:21 uses the identical phrase for God, strengthening the reading that this child shares in full deity.

options.text: It simply means a strong human warrior with no divine implication.
options.note: El, the word for God, makes this more than ordinary royal hyperbole about strength.

options.text: It was a common title given to every Davidic king.
options.note: This specific combination is distinctive to this passage, not a standard royal formula applied to every king.

options.text: It refers only to angelic beings, not to God or the Messiah.
options.note: El gibbor is used of the LORD Himself in Isaiah 10:21, not of angels.

quiz.q: What guarantees that the child's government and peace will have no end, according to verse 7?

options.text: The zeal of the LORD of Hosts will accomplish it.
options.note: The passage explicitly attributes the certainty of fulfillment to God's own passionate commitment, not human effort.

options.text: The strength of Judah's standing army.
options.note: The guarantee named is divine zeal, not military strength.

options.text: A treaty with a foreign power.
options.note: No treaty is mentioned; the guarantee is explicitly the LORD's own zeal.

options.text: The wisdom of Israel's existing royal counselors.
options.note: The passage credits God's own zeal, not the wisdom of existing human advisors.

quiz.q: On what basis will the child establish and sustain His kingdom, according to verse 7?

options.text: Justice and righteousness, mishpat and tzedaqah.
options.note: These are named explicitly as the foundation of His everlasting reign.

options.text: Military conquest alone.
options.note: The text names justice and righteousness, not conquest, as the foundation.

options.text: Wealth and trade agreements.
options.note: The passage centers on moral and relational categories, not economic policy.

options.text: Strict enforcement of ceremonial law.
options.note: Justice and righteousness are broader ethical categories than ceremonial regulation specifically.

quiz.q: How does Luke's Gospel echo Isaiah 9:7 in its announcement of Jesus's birth?

options.text: Gabriel tells Mary that Jesus will reign on David's throne forever, echoing the promise of an endless Davidic kingdom.
options.note: Luke 1:32-33 closely parallels the language of an everlasting Davidic reign found in Isaiah 9:7.

options.text: Luke makes no connection between Jesus and the Davidic throne.
options.note: Luke 1:32-33 explicitly ties Jesus to David's everlasting throne.

options.text: Luke applies this promise instead to King Herod.
options.note: The angel's announcement is specifically about Jesus, not Herod.

options.text: Luke connects this promise only to a future, distant descendant not yet born.
options.note: The announcement in Luke is specifically about Mary's own son, Jesus, being born imminently.

questions: This chapter answers deep darkness with the promise of a great light. Where in your life right now do you most need to remember that God's light has already dawned in Christ?
The child's name includes Wonderful Counselor. Where do you need His counsel right now more than your own plans or the advice of others?
He is called Everlasting Father, a King whose fatherly care never runs out. How does that shape the way you bring your ongoing needs and fears to Him?
The chapter promises the zeal of the LORD, not human effort, guarantees this everlasting kingdom. Where are you trying to secure by your own striving what only God's zeal can actually accomplish?
His government is built on justice and righteousness that will never end. How does trusting that ultimate justice is coming change the way you respond to injustice you cannot fix right now?

translationNotes.note: The fourfold name is sometimes punctuated differently across translations, for example 'Wonderful, Counselor' as two titles rather than one; the BSB and most modern versions follow the reading of four combined titles, Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace.

'Everlasting Father,' avi ad, does not imply the child is the first person of the Trinity, the Father, but describes His eternal, fatherly care and rule as King, consistent with the New Testament's clear distinction between the Father and the Son.

'Zeal,' qinah, can also carry the sense of jealousy, God's passionate, exclusive commitment to His own purposes and covenant promises, the same word used of God's jealousy for His people's exclusive worship elsewhere in Scripture.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from the Makor scripture toolkit for Isaiah 9:1-7.
